summary: Julia Evans argues that real-world machine learning work is fundamentally different from Kaggle competitions. While Kaggle focuses on optimizing algorithms with clean data and pre-defined metrics, actual ML work involves understanding business problems, choosing appropriate metrics, collecting and cleaning messy data, deploying models to production, and monitoring ongoing performance. She shares her own experience of feeling demoralized by Kaggle leaderboards despite doing ML professionally, and encourages others not to equate Kaggle performance with ML competence.
key_points: Kaggle abstracts away most of the hard parts of real ML work: business understanding, data collection, cleaning, deployment, and monitoring
Data cleaning alone can take 80% of your time when reconciling multiple uncontrolled data sources
Understanding the business problem matters more than model quality — if you don't know how the model fits into business plans, accuracy is irrelevant
Production deployment introduces entirely new challenges, as Netflix demonstrated by never implementing their competition-winning model because it was too complicated
The choice of metric to optimize is itself a substantive decision that requires domain understanding, not just statistical convenience
money_quotes: I still don't think I could win a Kaggle competition. But I have a job where I do (among other things) machine learning! What gives?
I've spent time on projects where I didn't understand at all how the model was going to fit into business plans. If this is you, it doesn't matter how good your model is. At all.
Cleaning up data to the point where you can work with it is a huge amount of work. If you're trying to reconcile a lot of sources of data that you don't control, it can take 80% of your time.
Netflix didn't actually implement the model that won the Netflix competition because it was too complicated.
Getting historical weather data is one thing, but getting that same data in real time to predict flight delays right now is totally different.

So if you look at Kaggle leaderboards and think that you're bad at machine learning because you're not doing well, don't. It's a fun but artificial problem that doesn't reflect real machine learning work.
key_insight: Real machine learning work is dominated by business understanding, data wrangling, and production engineering — the algorithm optimization that Kaggle tests is only a small slice of the job.
